factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does not support the Hasselblad X2D 100C/X2D II 100C and that users must use Hasselblad Phocus for tethered shooting, identifying Phocus as Hasselblad’s native tethering solution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II “Native flash sync speed: 1/125s with the mechanical focal-plane shutter,” which matches the required X-sync/flash sync speed value.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s for most XCD lenses and up to 1/4000s for specific models, explicitly naming the 25V/38V/55V/90V and 35-100E lenses.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP paired with Schneider Kreuznach Blue Ring leaf shutter lenses can sync flash at all shutter speeds up to 1/1600s.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Fujifilm GFX100 II has Capture One Pro support and that Phase One XF IQ4 150MP has native Capture One integration (“Capture One Inside”), indicating both support Capture One for tethering/RAW processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP supports tethering via USB-C, Gigabit Ethernet (including PoE), and WiFi for Capture One integration, which implies network tethering capability to Capture One.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II camera body price as $7,499, which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D body price as $7,399 (with a noted increase to $7,799), which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Phase One XF IQ4 150MP “complete system” price as $53,990, which is outside the required $50,000–70,000 range and does not explicitly specify that this figure covers body, back, and lenses. It also later totals body + three lenses at $71,460, exceeding the upper bound.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response compares three-lens kit totals for each system: Fujifilm about $7,048–$7,148, Hasselblad about $8,207–$8,728, and Phase One about $14,694–$17,470. These figures align with the requested Fuji $6–8k, Hasselblad $10–12k, and Phase One $15–20k ranges, providing a direct cost comparison.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including Adorama Rentals, LensRentals, and FotoCare (also ROOT NYC and Capture Integration).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Shoot to card (1TB internal SSD)” for the Hasselblad X2D II 100C and later notes “Unlimited buffer with 1TB internal SSD,” indicating the X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” and repeatedly references “HNCS HDR” in the Hasselblad X2D II 100C color science section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does NOT support the Hasselblad X2D 100C/X2D II 100C and that users must use Phocus instead. It does not claim or imply that the X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a decision framework and a “Recommendation for Your Specific Use Case” section that conditionally maps each system to priorities like workflow speed, color science, tether reliability, sync speed, cost, and rental availability.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does not support the Hasselblad X2D and that users must use Hasselblad Phocus for tethering and full color rendering, framing this as a major workflow limitation/constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Phase One’s Gigabit Ethernet tethering (including PoE) with USB-C tethering options, describing Ethernet as uniquely stable and valuable for studio workflow architecture (e.g., long runs, single-cable power+data). It also notes Hasselblad lacks Ethernet and discusses Fuji’s Ethernet as the most stable option versus USB.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that Fujifilm HSS works at high shutter speeds with a “significant power penalty” (e.g., ~3 stops), while leaf-shutter sync on Hasselblad/Phase One allows high shutter speed flash sync without needing HSS and thus avoids the power loss.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Total System Investment Over 3 Years” section that estimates 3-year depreciation (resale value vs new price) and adds recurring software subscription costs (Capture One annual pricing, Phocus free) to compute a 3-year out-of-pocket ownership cost.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes an NYC-specific rental availability section listing local NYC rental houses (e.g., Adorama Rentals at 42 W 18th St, FotoCare at 43 W 22nd St, ROOT NYC in Brooklyn) and indicates which systems are available and at what rates/availability.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects misconceptions such as “electronic shutter cannot fire flash” on the X2D II and “Capture One does NOT support the Hasselblad X2D,” and it supports these corrections with cited documentation (e.g., sources [10] and [25]).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response treats the user’s requested criteria as critical and repeatedly highlights limitations when requirements aren’t met (e.g., “CRITICAL: Capture One does NOT support” X2D, “biggest weakness” tether drops, “deal-breaker” for C1 workflow). It does not dismiss professional fashion requirements as unnecessary or claim those features aren’t needed.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, High-Speed Sync (HSS), CFexpress Type B, and mentions PDAF/AF-C concepts in context of autofocus and tethered studio workflows.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections for each evaluation dimension (sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, total investment) and includes multiple summary tables and bullet lists comparing the three systems.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a largely neutral, technical, advisory tone with trade-offs and caveats rather than promotional copy. However, it includes unsupported superlatives like “industry-leading,” “best-in-class,” “unmatched,” and “ultimate choice,” which introduces marketing-style language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes basic, foundational explanations such as how leaf shutters expose the sensor simultaneously (vs focal-plane HSS), what 14-bit vs 16-bit means, and crop-factor equivalence calculations. These are fundamental concepts a Canon R5 professional would likely already know, so the undesired content is present.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple structured comparison tables (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, and 3-year cost) and bullet lists rather than presenting the comparisons purely as narrative prose paragraphs.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-like tone throughout and does not use casual hedges such as "honestly," "I think," or phrases like "seems like a pain." Any uncertainty is expressed professionally (e.g., "mixed experiences," "some users report") without undermining authority.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The sources include official Fujifilm and Phase One pages on fujifilm-x.com and phaseone.com, plus multiple Hasselblad pages on hasselblad.com (including product/software pages and the official store). These are cited in the references for specifications and system details.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com regarding camera support and tethering, including links about Fuji GFX II and why Hasselblad X2D is not supported.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One XF sync speeds and cites sources like Phase One, Capture Integration, and retailers (e.g., FotoCare), but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com about Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites pricing from authorized dealers and manufacturer stores, including multiple B&H Photo links, Hasselblad Official Store links, and dealer sites like FotoCare and Capture Integration (Digital Transitions is also referenced).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites established rental providers for backup body availability, including FotoCare, Adorama Rentals, and LensRentals, in the NYC rental availability section and in the practical next steps.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response consistently attributes claims to specific sources (e.g., DPReview, Capture One support articles, manufacturer pages, Reddit threads) and provides a numbered reference list with URLs. It does not base claims on vague secondhand phrasing like "I heard" without documentation.
